subject: AP US Government and Politics
theme: The American system of government was built on Enlightenment ideals of natural rights and limited government, structured through federalism and the Constitution to balance order with liberty.
core_concepts: The Declaration of Independence (1776) articulated natural rights, popular sovereignty, and the social contract — ideas drawn from John Locke that justified revolution against tyranny.
The Articles of Confederation created a weak central government with no executive branch, no taxing power, and no ability to regulate commerce — weaknesses exposed by Shays' Rebellion (1786-87).
The Constitution established a federal system with separation of powers across legislative, executive, and judicial branches, each checked by the others to prevent tyranny (Federalist No. 51).
Federalists (Hamilton, Madison) argued a large republic controls factions (Federalist No. 10); Anti-Federalists (Brutus No. 1) warned that consolidated power in a large republic threatens liberty.
Federalism divides power between national and state governments: enumerated powers belong to Congress, reserved powers belong to states (10th Amendment), and concurrent powers are shared.
The Necessary and Proper Clause and Commerce Clause have expanded federal power over time, while the 10th Amendment and cases like United States v. Lopez (1995) have preserved state authority.

Fiscal federalism uses categorical grants (specific rules), block grants (fewer restrictions), and unfunded mandates to shape the federal-state relationship through financial incentives and requirements.
McCulloch v. Maryland (1819) established implied powers and federal supremacy, ruling that Congress can create a national bank and states cannot tax federal institutions.

key_terms.definition: Rights inherent to all people (life, liberty, property) that government cannot grant or take away — central to the Declaration of Independence.
Agreement between people and government: citizens consent to be governed in exchange for protection of their natural rights.
The principle that government authority derives from the consent of the governed.
A system dividing power between a national government and state governments, each sovereign in their own sphere.
Constitutional division of government into legislative, executive, and judicial branches with distinct functions.
System allowing each branch to limit the others — e.g., presidential veto, Senate confirmation, judicial review.
Powers explicitly granted to Congress in Article I, Section 8 (e.g., coin money, declare war, regulate commerce).
Powers not stated in the Constitution but inferred from the Necessary and Proper Clause (e.g., creating a national bank).
Powers kept by the states under the 10th Amendment (e.g., education, marriage laws, driver's licenses).
Article VI provision establishing that the Constitution and federal laws override conflicting state laws.
Article I, Section 8 'elastic clause' giving Congress power to make all laws necessary for executing its enumerated powers.
Gives Congress power to regulate interstate and foreign trade — broadly interpreted to expand federal authority.
Madison's argument that a large republic dilutes factions, preventing any one group from dominating.
Anti-Federalist warning that a large republic concentrates power and distances representatives from the people.
Madison's case for separation of powers: 'Ambition must be made to counteract ambition.'
Federal funds given to states for specific purposes with strict conditions on spending.
Federal funds given to states for broad purposes with few restrictions, favored by conservatives for state flexibility.
Federal requirements imposed on states without providing the funds to implement them.

exam_tip: The AP exam frequently asks you to compare Federalist and Anti-Federalist arguments — always connect Federalist No. 10 (factions controlled by large republic) vs. Brutus No. 1 (large republic threatens liberty) to earn full credit on FRQs.
